name: Badarayana
dates_notes: Date range spans four centuries; identity with Vyāsa is traditional, not academic; only the Brahma-Sūtra survives, and even its layers may be multi-author.
core_thesis: Bādarāyaṇa systematizes Upaniṣadic teaching into a four-*adhyāya* aphoristic argument whose declared aim (1.1.1: *athāto brahma-jijñāsā*) is *brahma-jijñāsā* — inquiry into *brahman* as the one *jagat-kāraṇa* (cause of the world; 1.1.2: *janmādy asya yataḥ*). The text proceeds by *samanvaya* (harmonization of śruti texts on *brahman*), *avirodha* (rebuttal of rival cosmologies — Sāṅkhya *pradhāna*, Vaiśeṣika atomism, Buddhist momentariness), *sādhana* (means: meditative *upāsanā*, Vedic karma's relation to knowledge), and *phala* (the discipline's fruit: *mokṣa*, the post-mortem trajectory of the *jīva*). On the *jīva*–*brahman* relation Bādarāyaṇa cites earlier ācāryas (Auḍulomi, Āśmarathya, Kāśakṛtsna) without fully resolving their dispute — a deliberate underdetermination that licenses the later school-formation (Advaita, Viśiṣṭādvaita, Dvaita, Bhedābheda) each of which reads the same sūtras through its own *bhāṣya* lens. The work is the foundational *prasthāna* of all later Vedānta.

engaged_works.title: Brahma-Sūtra
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored as a compiled text; the work is multi-layered and likely incorporates earlier sūtra-material.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus accepts the Brahma-Sūtra as a unified compiled text traditionally ascribed to Bādarāyaṇa, with internal-style evidence of multi-layered sūtra-material.

engaged_works.summary: 555 *sūtras* in four *adhyāyas* (each with four *pādas*): *samanvaya* (1; harmonization of śruti as teaching *brahman*), *avirodha* (2; defense against rival schools — Sāṅkhya, Vaiśeṣika, Buddhist, Jaina, Pāśupata, Pāñcarātra), *sādhana* (3; *upāsanā*, the *jīva* in transmigration, the relation of *karma* to *vidyā*), *phala* (4; the path of the liberated knower — *devayāna*, *kramamukti*, the nature of the *mukta-puruṣa*). Internal citations to ācāryas Auḍulomi, Āśmarathya, Kāśakṛtsna, Bādari, Jaimini, and Kārṣṇājini show the work as the consolidation of an earlier oral *sūtra* tradition. Its compressed style is unintelligible without commentary; this very feature makes it the disputed common ground of every Vedāntic school. Securely a compiled work; the question of single vs. multi-author redaction is open. Modern critical edition: Thibaut (SBE), Deussen, and the Ānandāśrama editions of the major *bhāṣyas*.
